questions.question: What does the acronym SPOOL stand for?
questions.option_a: A. System Performance Optimization Online
questions.option_b: B. Simultaneous Peripheral Operation Online
questions.option_c: C. Standard Processing of Output Logs
questions.option_d: D. Shared Program Object Orientation Layer
questions.explanation: SPOOL is an acronym for Simultaneous Peripheral Operation Online, as stated in the lecture.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using SPOOL?
questions.option_a: A. To increase the size of main memory
questions.option_b: B. To keep both I/O and CPU busy all the time
questions.option_c: C. To provide a graphical user interface
questions.option_d: D. To secure the system from viruses
questions.explanation: SPOOLing allows I/O and CPU to operate concurrently, keeping both busy and improving overall system utilization.

questions.question: In a multiprogramming environment, what data structure allows the OS to select the job that runs next?
questions.option_a: A. The cache memory
questions.option_b: B. The interrupt vector
questions.option_c: C. The job pool
questions.option_d: D. The system call table
questions.explanation: The job pool is a data structure that holds multiple jobs, enabling the OS to choose which one to execute next.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a service the operating system must consider to support multiprogramming?
questions.option_a: A. Job scheduling
questions.option_b: B. Memory management
questions.option_c: C. CPU scheduling
questions.option_d: D. SPOOLing management
questions.explanation: SPOOLing is a technique, not a core service like job scheduling, memory management, CPU scheduling, or I/O allocation.

questions.question: How does multiprogramming increase CPU utilization?
questions.option_a: A. By keeping only one program in memory at a time
questions.option_b: B. By keeping several programs in memory and multiplexing the CPU among them
questions.option_c: C. By increasing the clock speed of the CPU
questions.option_d: D. By using a faster hard disk
questions.explanation: Multiprogramming keeps multiple programs in memory; while one waits for I/O, the CPU switches to another, keeping the CPU busy.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of a time-sharing system?
questions.option_a: A. It is only used for batch processing
questions.option_b: B. It provides interactive use of the computer system at a reasonable cost
questions.option_c: C. It does not require memory management
questions.option_d: D. It runs only one program at a time
questions.explanation: Time-sharing systems enable multiple users to interact with the system simultaneously, providing interactive computing at reasonable cost.

questions.question: In a hard real-time system, what is the consequence if a response is not generated within the allocated time?
questions.option_a: A. There will be a smaller, manageable amount of damage
questions.option_b: B. The system simply slows down
questions.option_c: C. There will be a larger amount of damage created
questions.option_d: D. The user is prompted to try again
questions.explanation: Missing a deadline in a hard real-time system can lead to catastrophic system failure or significant damage.

questions.question: Which type of real-time system is commonly used in controlling scientific experiments and medical imaging systems?
questions.option_a: A. Soft real-time system
questions.option_b: B. Time-sharing system
questions.option_c: C. Batch system
questions.option_d: D. Hard real-time system
questions.explanation: Hard real-time systems are used in applications like scientific experiments and medical imaging where timing constraints are critical.

questions.question: Storage systems are organized in a hierarchy based on which criteria?
questions.option_a: A. Color and manufacturer
questions.option_b: B. Speed, cost, volatility, and size/capacity
questions.option_c: C. Age and version
questions.option_d: D. Number of users and applications
questions.explanation: The storage hierarchy is organized by trade-offs in speed, cost, volatility, and capacity.

questions.question: What is the bootstrap program?
questions.option_a: A. A type of application software
questions.option_b: B. A program loaded at power-up or reboot that initializes the system
questions.option_c: C. A user interface for managing files
questions.option_d: D. A hardware component for cooling the CPU
questions.explanation: The bootstrap program is the first code executed when a computer starts; it initializes the system and loads the OS kernel.

questions.question: Where is the bootstrap program typically stored?
questions.option_a: A. On the hard disk
questions.option_b: B. In RAM
questions.option_c: C. In ROM or EPROM (firmware)
questions.option_d: D. In the CPU cache
questions.explanation: The bootstrap program resides in non-volatile memory such as ROM or EPROM, often called firmware.

questions.question: A system that contains only one CPU to execute general-purpose instructions is called a:
questions.option_a: A. Multiprocessor system
questions.option_b: B. Clustered system
questions.option_c: C. Single-processor system
questions.option_d: D. Multicore system
questions.explanation: A single-processor system has exactly one general-purpose CPU.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as an advantage of a multiprocessor system?
questions.option_a: A. Increased throughput
questions.option_b: B. Economy of scale
questions.option_c: C. Increased reliability
questions.option_d: D. Reduced power consumption
questions.explanation: Advantages include increased throughput, economy of scale, and increased reliability; reduced power consumption is not a primary advantage.

questions.question: In Asymmetric Multiprocessing (AMP), how are the processors organized?
questions.option_a: A. All processors are equal and share the workload
questions.option_b: B. With a master-slave approach
questions.option_c: C. Each processor runs its own independent OS
questions.option_d: D. They are connected via a slow network
questions.explanation: Asymmetric multiprocessing uses a master-slave relationship: a master processor controls and schedules work for the slave processors.

questions.question: A key characteristic of Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP) is:
questions.option_a: A. It uses a master processor to control others
questions.option_b: B. It has no master-slave model and each processor runs an identical copy of the OS
questions.option_c: C. It is only used in extremely large systems
questions.option_d: D. Each processor has its own separate memory not shared with others
questions.explanation: In SMP, all processors are peers; each runs an identical copy of the OS and shares memory.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a clustered system and a multiprocessor system?
questions.option_a: A. A clustered system consists of individual systems or nodes connected via LAN
questions.option_b: B. A clustered system has only one CPU
questions.option_c: C. A multiprocessor system cannot share storage
questions.option_d: D. A clustered system does not offer high-availability
questions.explanation: Clustered systems are loosely coupled, composed of independent nodes connected via a local area network, unlike tightly coupled multiprocessors.

questions.question: According to the lecture, which operating system is an example of a simple structure?
questions.option_a: A. UNIX
questions.option_b: B. Linux
questions.option_c: C. Mac OS
questions.option_d: D. MS-DOS
questions.explanation: MS-DOS is cited as an example of a simple operating system structure with little separation between components.

questions.question: Which operating system is given as an example of a layered structure?
questions.option_a: A. MS-DOS
questions.option_b: B. UNIX
questions.option_c: C. An abstraction (layered)
questions.option_d: D. Solaris
questions.explanation: A layered operating system is an abstraction where the system is divided into hierarchical layers, each using services of lower layers.

questions.question: According to the lecture, what is a key characteristic of the MS-DOS structure?
questions.option_a: A. It is divided into many well-separated modules
questions.option_b: B. Its interfaces and levels of functionality are not well separated
questions.option_c: C. It is based on a microkernel
questions.option_d: D. It is designed for multiprocessor systems
questions.explanation: MS-DOS has a simple structure where interfaces and functionality levels are not clearly separated, leading to minimal modularity.

questions.question: In the traditional UNIX structure, what does the kernel consist of?
questions.option_a: A. Only the user interface
questions.option_b: B. Everything below the system-call interface and above the physical hardware
questions.option_c: C. Only the hardware drivers
questions.option_d: D. The system libraries
questions.explanation: The UNIX kernel includes all system components between the system-call interface and the hardware.

questions.question: In a layered operating system structure, how do the layers interact?
questions.option_a: A. Each layer can use functions from any other layer
questions.option_b: B. Each layer uses functions and services of only lower-level layers
questions.option_c: C. The top layer communicates directly with the hardware
questions.option_d: D. Layers operate completely independently
questions.explanation: Layered design enforces that each layer can only use services provided by lower layers, promoting modularity and abstraction.

questions.question: What is a primary benefit of a microkernel structure?
questions.option_a: A. It has a large amount of code running in kernel mode
questions.option_b: B. It is more reliable because less code is running in kernel mode
questions.option_c: C. It provides the most functionality in the least space
questions.option_d: D. It has no performance overhead
questions.explanation: A microkernel minimizes kernel-mode code, reducing the chance of system crashes and improving reliability.

questions.question: What is a detriment or disadvantage of the microkernel approach?
questions.option_a: A. It is difficult to extend
questions.option_b: B. It is hard to port to new architectures
questions.option_c: C. Performance overhead of user space to kernel space communication
questions.option_d: D. It cannot use message passing
questions.explanation: The frequent communication between user-mode modules and the microkernel via message passing introduces performance overhead.

questions.question: How do modules communicate in a microkernel-based system?
questions.option_a: A. By directly accessing each other's memory
questions.option_b: B. By using system calls only
questions.option_c: C. Using message passing
questions.option_d: D. Through a shared global variable
questions.explanation: Microkernel architectures use message passing as the primary communication mechanism between user-space modules and the kernel.

questions.question: Modern operating systems like Linux and Solaris implement which type of structure?
questions.option_a: A. Simple structure
questions.option_b: B. Layered structure only
questions.option_c: C. Microkernel structure only
questions.option_d: D. Loadable kernel modules
questions.explanation: Linux, Solaris, and many modern OSes use loadable kernel modules to dynamically add functionality, combining modularity with performance.
